intro: 2004. Lego is basically bankrupt. Years of chasing theme parks, clothing lines, video games, and increasingly cursed mega-sets buried the company in complexity and debt. You just became the first CEO from outside the founding family. Everyone forgot the little brick that built the whole thing.

realOutcome.valuation: became the world's largest toymaker, valued at $15B+
realOutcome.summary: Lego went from nearly bankrupt to the most profitable toy company on Earth. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: Lego has sprawled into theme parks, apparel, video games, and thousands of specialized products, losing hundreds of millions a year. Double down on being a 'lifestyle brand' — or go back to the brick?

choices.text: Refocus on the core brick, cut the sprawl
choices.reveal: Knudstorp took Lego back to basics — the brick and building play — and axed the distractions. Remembering what Lego is actually FOR became the spine of the whole turnaround. Touch grass, find brick.

choices.text: Keep expanding into parks, clothes, and games
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You chase 'lifestyle brand' while losing money on all of it. Spread across a dozen industries you don't understand, Lego runs dry defending its own confusion. Diworsification.

choices.text: Sell the whole company to a rival toymaker
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You hand a beloved brand to a conglomerate that strips it for parts. The magic that could've been revived dies in a spreadsheet. Rip Bozo.

decisions.scenario: You need cash AND focus. The Legoland parks are capital-hungry and nowhere near your core. Sell them to raise money, or keep running an amusement-park business?

choices.text: Sell the Legoland parks to raise cash
choices.reveal: Lego sold a majority stake in the Legoland parks to private equity in 2005. Raised cash and let the company get back to making toys instead of running roller coasters. Smart offload.

choices.text: Keep the parks, borrow more to fund them
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You take on more debt to run a capital-hungry business you're not built for. The parks drain the exact cash the toy turnaround needs. Money pit.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You double down on parks while the toy business is dying. Expanding a distraction is not the same as fixing the core, chief.

decisions.scenario: Your designers keep inventing unique specialized pieces — the catalog ballooned to ~13,000 different elements — and the cost and complexity are crushing you.

choices.text: Halve the number of unique pieces
choices.reveal: Lego cut its unique elements from ~13,000 toward 7,000. Fewer parts = lower cost, simpler manufacturing, and pieces reusable across tons of sets. Less is more, unironically.

choices.text: Keep every specialty piece for creative freedom
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You protect designer freedom AND the runaway complexity that nearly bankrupted you. Every new bespoke piece is another bill you can't pay.

choices.text: Outsource design to cut costs faster
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cut cost by cutting quality and control. The thing that makes Lego LEGO — the precision and craft — starts slipping. Enshittification incoming.

decisions.scenario: There's an audience Lego treated as a footnote: adult superfans, and licensed sets tied to franchises like Star Wars. Lean into them, or stay strictly a little-kids toy company?

choices.text: Lean into licensing and adult superfans
choices.reveal: Lego embraced licensed sets and its obsessive adult fanbase, even crowdsourcing designs via Lego Ideas. Those grown-up fans and franchises became a huge high-margin engine. AFOLs carried the team.
ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You ignore the adults dropping hundreds on giant sets and the franchises that pull them in. You leave your most profitable customers on read.

choices.text: Drop all licenses to save on royalties
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cut Star Wars and other licenses to dodge royalties — and lose the sets that bring millions of fans to the brand. Saved pennies, lost the fandom.

decisions.scenario: A movie studio pitches a full feature film built entirely around Lego. Could be a beloved blockbuster — or a 90-minute toy commercial that cheapens the whole brand.

choices.reveal: The Lego Movie (2014) was a critical and commercial smash — 'Everything Is Awesome' lived in your head rent-free — that celebrated creativity and drove massive set sales. The risky film became one of the best marketing moves ever.

choices.text: Keep the brand off the big screen to stay safe
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You play it safe and pass on a cultural moment. A great film that could've introduced Lego to a whole new generation just never gets made. Played it too safe.

choices.text: License the film out and lose creative control
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You let someone else make a cheap Lego movie with zero care for the brand. It becomes the exact 90-minute ad you were scared of. Self-fulfilling prophecy.

bands.line: You went back to the brick and rebuilt an empire. Legend episode: WON. Everything is awesome.
Stabilized and profitable, never the world's #1 toymaker. Mid.
Diversified into bankruptcy. The brick forgotten. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Lego 10x'd by returning to its core. Today's agents flagged a company that diversified itself into a crisis →
